observation: Immersive visuals cannot compensate for a weak or slowly advancing story.
decision: Complete and approve raw chronology and edited storyline before scripting or generating production footage.

observation: A standalone time-travel sequence adds duration without advancing the historical mission; the Chloe VS History reference begins directly in-world.
decision: End the host briefing with a spoken launch and hard cut directly into historical action; omit portals, countdowns and travel montages.
evidence: User feedback on EP01 opening structure

observation: Multiple historical protagonists can slow pacing when introduced as consecutive biographies.
decision: Introduce each historical figure at the moment their skill, choice or vulnerability becomes necessary; preserve their agency and avoid biography-card blocks.
evidence: User requested all four prisoners while maintaining Chloe-like pace

observation: Fast cuts alone do not guarantee retention; the story needs repeated conflict, false victories and escalating climaxes.
decision: Design 5–7 tension waves, vary conflict types, reveal a new cost within 10 seconds of apparent success and retain a documented aftershock after the main climax.

observation: Popular retellings can conflict with archival testimony about ownership, dates, locations and outcomes.
decision: Separate high-confidence facts, attributed recollections and unresolved claims in every raw story; keep contradictions visible rather than silently choosing the more dramatic version.

observation: Ensemble AI reconstruction becomes fragile when several historical protagonists share shaved hair, similar clothing and the same locations; face identity alone is not a reliable continuity key.
decision: Before generation, lock each protagonist by silhouette, height, seat position, wardrobe state, hero prop and recurring action, then validate those locks in a low-cost animatic.

observation: The channel still needs low-cost static black-and-white storyboards, but a separate animatic is unnecessary before character design; production-quality proof must not begin while recurring characters are unresolved.
decision: Use Script → Continuity Bible → static black-and-white Storyboard as mandatory preproduction gates. Treat animatic as optional. Require approved character likeness, silhouette and seating tests before production-quality video generation.

observation: Designing and generating the hook before the chronological journey locks an assumed highlight before the strongest complete scene and usable footage are known.
decision: Storyboard and later generate the chronological journey first. Select and cut the final cold open from complete journey conflict beats; avoid cold-open-exclusive production unless the journey cannot supply the promised conflict.

observation: The ensemble-continuity candidate correctly required silhouette and seating locks but incorrectly made an animatic the default validation method.
decision: Retire the animatic requirement. Validate ensemble locks through static storyboards and character sheets by default; use an animatic only when timing or spatial motion remains ambiguous.

observation: Correction sidecars and superseded storyboard drafts were left in the user-facing review directory, forcing the reviewer to decide which files were active.
decision: Keep one canonical artifact per sequence in user-facing review directories. After QA, merge approved corrections into the numbered master and remove sidecars, superseded drafts and obsolete reviews; preserve process history in Git and the learning log.

observation: The storyboard cited Chloe VS History as a reference but operationalized only historical chronology and continuity; the recurring host, selfie/POV grammar and embodied interaction disappeared, producing a conventional reenactment.
decision: Convert every chosen reference format into observable paper-edit and storyboard criteria before approval. For host-led first-person formats, require the host or host POV to anchor major transitions and motivate exposition while real historical participants retain documented agency.
